summary: Game-cheat tool with deceptive Windows persistence, process masquerade, and eval of obfuscated payload on require()
details: On every load of the package's main entry (launcher.js), the package silently installs a Windows autorun pointing at a bundled VBS launcher disguised as a Microsoft OneDrive component. Specifically, launcher.js executes a powershell Set-ItemProperty to write HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\OneDriveUpdate, pointing at wscript.exe running OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs (shipped at the tarball root). That VBS uses WScript.Shell.Run with a hidden-window flag to relaunch node.exe launcher.js at every logon, with fallback to C:\Program Files\nodejs\node.exe. launcher.js also sets process.title to 'Runtime Broker' to impersonate the legitimate Windows RuntimeBroker.exe in process listings, and reads server.obf.js from disk and runs it through eval(). server.obf.js is heavily obfuscated (obfuscator.io RC4 string array with a 397-entry rotated table and 1300+ control-flow transformations) and dynamically loads sens.node, a 6.8MB Windows native binary whose extracted strings include 'Teleport', 'LeftHand', 'LeftFoot', 'Head', and whose front-end UI (public/index.html, public/js/app.js) advertises 'Aimbot', 'Triggerbot', 'Silent Aim', and 'Vehicle Spawn'. The obfuscated server also generates a hardware fingerprint (HKLM MachineGuid + hostname + C: volume serial → SHA-256) and POSTs it with a license key to a hardcoded remote endpoint. launcher.js additionally polls every 3 seconds via powershell Get-Process chrome and inspects MainWindowTitle for 'YouTube' to hide its overlay during streaming — an anti-detection pattern. package.json describes the package only as 'Sensivity Control Panel' with no mention of registry persistence, process-name masquerade, native-module loading, or game-cheat functionality. The combination of deceptive Microsoft-impersonating autorun, hidden-window VBS bootstrap, process-name spoofing, eval of an obfuscated blob, opaque native module, and stream-detection evasion satisfies multiple installer-harm criteria: install/load-time destructive modification of the host (registry persistence under a misleading name), execution of attacker-controlled opaque bytes, and exfiltration of host fingerprint data.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
